data.title: Healthcare Exposure Focused On Big Pharma? You're Missing Out
data.description:
By Rafael Silva

The healthcare sector is undergoing a profound transformation, driven by rapid technological advancements and shifting global demographics. The leaders of this transformation go far beyond the big pharmaceutical names that dominate healthcare strategies. For investors seeking targeted exposure to this evolution, the ROBO Global Healthcare Technology & Innovation Index (HTEC) provides a specialized, research-driven benchmark.

Investing in Solutions, Not Just Sectors

The index is designed to capture the entire value chain of disruptive and emerging healthcare technologies. It does this by moving away from traditional, broad sector classifications like “pharmaceuticals” or “healthcare providers,” and instead utilizing forward-looking subsegments to cover massive structural trends, from breakthroughs in new drug development to the systemic challenges of an aging population.

Ultimately, the process is a bottoms-up approach. The team identifies societal needs, determines what innovation is needed to address them, and that informs the creation of the subsegments, which are the actual fields able to provide the required solutions.

9 Key Subsegments Solving Critical Healthcare Challenges

Take the aging population as an example of this methodology in action. The latest U.S. Census projects that by the mid-2030s, older adults will outnumber children for the first time in U.S. history. As the global population ages, the strain on the healthcare system increases alongside physician shortages. Notably, the AAMC projects a shortfall of up to 86,000 physicians by 2036. HTEC captures solutions to this specific structural trend by including companies in segments like Robotics (surgical robots that bring improved outcomes and shorter recovery times), Medical Instruments (devices for chronic illnesses), and Telehealth (expanding access).

Healthcare Technology: From Luxury to Necessity

There’s a realization that healthcare technology is transitioning from a luxury to an absolute necessity. Challenges like unsustainably growing healthcare costs require technology to be properly addressed. U.S. national health expenditures were projected to reach $5.6 trillion in 2025 and $8.6 trillion by 2033, growing at nearly 5.8% annually and outpacing GDP growth. Technologies like AI diagnostics, telehealth, and robotics are uniquely positioned to solve these structural issues by increasing efficiency and drastically lowering costs.

But at the end of the day, investors care about strong balance sheets and execution. Recent years have seen a combination of important regulatory approvals in areas like precision medicine and genomics, alongside massive leaps in technology supporting AI-driven drug discovery. This, coupled with significantly reduced concerns regarding political regulations, has allowed companies to concentrate on executing their strategies and bringing treatments to the population.

However, because this space is evolving so rapidly, there is a widening gap between the true innovators and the legacy companies lagging behind. Identifying the companies with the technological edge and execution capabilities to actually deliver these treatments requires deep, specialized research. A passive approach simply won’t catch them in time.

Under the Hood: Constituent Selection and Scoring

To capitalize on these trends, a passive, set-it-and-forget-it approach is rarely enough. Healthcare innovation moves incredibly fast. For that reason, the HTEC index methodology is built specifically to stay agile, using a blend of quantitative metrics and deep qualitative research to select constituents. To be eligible for inclusion, a company must have a market cap above $200 million and a trailing 3-month average daily trading volume (ADTV) of at least $2 million.

Each company is then individually analyzed and assigned a custom score ranging from 1 to 100. This score is based on the level of revenue a company receives from innovative healthcare technologies, a qualitative and quantitative assessment of the company’s competitive positioning, technological edge, and market leadership, as well as the level of investment that the firm is actively making in its technologies, which indicates its commitment to future growth.

A company must have an aggregate score of 50 or higher to be considered for inclusion. This score also underpins the modified equal-weight model of the index. Unlike typical market-cap-weighted strategies where the weight is dictated simply by the size of the company, this model ensures that the strategy allocates more weight to the leading companies with the highest exposure to the theme.

Dynamic Weighting and Portfolio Agility

The modified equal-weighting scheme also adjusts every quarter. This means that not only is the index able to add and drop companies, but it can also adjust its exposure to reflect the constituents’ revenue purity, investment levels, and their market and technological leadership. This way, the strategy is always ensuring that it is overweighting the leading companies while rapidly absorbing new market entrants (like recent IPOs) without waiting for an annual review.

On top of that, the team is constantly updating the roadmap to make sure that the subsegments are relevant, hold the best companies, and effectively capture the entire value chain. After all, one of the key benefits of investing in a research-based strategy like this is that it’s forward-looking. So, while there is the ability to make changes often, those changes are primarily made to reflect the future of healthcare, rather than simply reacting to short-term market signals.

Conclusion

Ultimately, investing in the future of healthcare requires more than just buying the biggest legacy names in a broad sector strategy. As demographic shifts and unsustainably rising costs force the medical industry to adapt, disruptive technology is no longer just a nice-to-have, it’s the only viable path forward. By relying on a dynamic, research-driven methodology rather than a static market-cap weighting, the ROBO Global Healthcare Technology & Innovation Index (HTEC) ensures it captures the companies actively solving these systemic issues. For investors looking to gain true exposure to the innovations shaping tomorrow’s medical landscape, moving beyond traditional sector classifications is a necessity.

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2026. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit www.vettafi.com

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Joby Aviation Stock Is Getting Ready For Takeoff
data.description:
With Joby Aviation, Inc. (JOBY) being one of the leaders in the next frontier of transportation, I find it quite surprising myself that I haven't initiated coverage on the stock yet since my analysis is mostly tech focused. Today, I have finally decided to provide an analysis on the company and I believe it comes at an important time. As you can see in the stock price chart below, JOBY stock has fallen nearly 50% in the past six months. Below, I will review their most recent earnings reported in late February and also discuss some important recent developments.

Below, it is shown that commercialization is nearer than ever before. Certification progress is significant and they are set for a Dubai launch later this year. While there are some risks such as further dilution and some legal risks with relation to China, the valuation's steep contraction makes the current setup opportunistic. Therefore, I'm initiating Joby stock with a Buy rating today.

Certification Not Far Away

We will get to some financial results later in this article but at this point those numbers may be secondary in importance. Let's first take a look at regulatory and commercialization progress. As you can see above, Joby has come a long way in terms of the certification process with the FAA. The company has fully completed Stage 1 and 3 while Stage 2 is on the cusp of completion. Meanwhile for Stage 4, on the FAA side they stated that they saw record progress as there was an 18 point advance.

For this industry, regulations are one of the large hurdles to commercialization and in my view the relative speed at which they are clearing these hurdles should be encouraging for investors. In early to mid March, Joby took a large step forward. It was reported that testing had begun for their first FAA-conforming electric air taxi aircraft. This has been called a key milestone and so the company continues to inch towards passenger service in the United States.

Uber Collaboration

Joby is set to start passenger services in Dubai later this year as they already announced the first few vertiports there. However, the even better signal that commercialization is near is their collaboration with Uber (UBER). Uber Air powered by Joby is a deep integration as Uber users will be able to book air taxis right in the Uber App. On the right, an illustration of what this could look like is shown and you can see that it is likely to be a seamless experience for users.

In my opinion, the willingness of Uber to collaborate with Joby shows that commercialization is really just around the corner. For Joby, being able to partner with one of the titans of the passenger transport industry is a vote of confidence and should help them to expand and scale as their services continue to mature. All in all, I like what I'm seeing as the company seems set to move away from the R&D phase.

The Numbers

For 2025 Q4, Joby reported a massive YoY increase in revenues. While their Blade Urban Air Mobility acquisition contributed to this strength, there are other factors like their Pentagon contracts. Nonetheless, their reported $30.84 million for the top line beat expectations by $13.95 million and even if these numbers aren't the most important right now, that is still an encouraging sign. As another promising sign, you can see above that Joby has narrowed their net loss YoY. Looking forward, the company expects to see between $105 million to $115 million of revenues for full year 2026. Therefore, even their numbers suggest that commercialization is near underway now.

Not long ago, Joby announced both an equity offering and a debt offering to fund their operations and capital investments. Of course, this means dilution for shareholders and moving forward that is still a risk. In the near term, I believe investors need not be too worried. As you can see above, in Q4 Joby's operations burned through $153.17 million in cash while FCF was at -$166.98 million. Cash burn has accelerated in recent times as the company gears up for commercial service but as of the end of Q4, they have $1.41 billion in cash and cash equivalents.

With their cash position being quite strong after the capital raise, further need for capital may not happen in the near future if the current cash burn rate continues. However, there are signs that cash burn could increase. Moving into 2027, the company aims to double production to address the opportunity at hand and they have signed an agreement to acquire a production facility near Ohio that's more than 700,000 square feet. To me, this sounds like a considerable need for cash to enable commercialization and so further dilution is far from being off the table in my view.

Trump Administration Is Supportive

While the FAA certification process isn't complete yet, the Trump Administration seems very supportive of the eVTOL industry. It is always nice to have the government on your side and so Joby may see an accelerated timeline in the United States. In early March, it was reported that Joby, along with seven other firms, was selected to take part in the Department of Transportation's air taxi pilot program. As Amy Thielen of Seeking Alpha writes

Under Executive Order 14307, Unleashing American Drone Dominance, President Trump declared that it is the policy of the U.S. to accelerate the safe commercialization of UAS and fully integrate UAS into the national airspace system, as well as eVTOL and other AAM aircraft operations.

Legal Risks

In addition to the dilution risk that I discussed earlier, Joby may have some legal risks to deal with. Back in March, it was reported that rival Archer Aviation (ACHR) had filed a countersuit against the company. Archer is accusing Joby of defrauding the U.S. Government and hiding a relationship with China. In response, Joby attorney Alex Spiro stated that

Archer’s constant legal issues and flailing business operations have left it no choice but to resort to invented nonsensical theories. We will see them in court.

At this point, it's anyone's guess how this lawsuit will turn out. Whatever the ultimate outcome may be, these events introduce potential legal risks for Joby and that may play a part in weighing on the stock's performance. Funny enough, this countersuit was reportedly filed on the same day that the DOT pilot program selection was announced. It seems that competition is getting ugly.

Valuation Seems Opportunistic

With the company's revenue only now slowly trickling in, the P/B ratio is likely the only valuation multiple that makes sense for Joby currently. As you can see in the chart above, the ratio has receded significantly from the highs of 2025. At the current reading of just 5.856, the multiple is just modestly above the three year median. In my view, this shows that the valuation is opportunistic.

In this article, it was shown that Joby is now nearer to commercialization than ever. Significant FAA certification progress has been made and through the Uber collaboration, the company seems ready to start passenger flights, starting with Dubai later this year. Meanwhile, the Trump Administration's supportive stance should accelerate their domestic efforts. While there are risks such as dilution and the Archer Aviation lawsuit, I believe the sharp contraction in the valuation provides investors with a good margin for error right now.

While a P/B reading of 5.856 isn't the cheapest in the world, given that eVOTL is the future of transport and that Joby is a leader in this rapidly expanding market, I would say that the valuation shows the risk/reward is favorable for long term investors.

Takeaway

Joby stock has been punished in the past few months. Judging by the stock performance, you might have thought that the company's growth story was getting derailed either due to regulatory pressures or technological roadblocks. In reality, the company seems on track to deliver on their mission. While there are always risks especially for a relatively young company like Joby, the valuation slide makes this a very attractive buying opportunity in my view. The stock seems to be getting ready for takeoff and so I have decided to initiate with a Buy rating now.

data.title: A Rough March For Gold As The Leading Precious Metal Searches For A Bottom
data.description:
March was a month in which a significant geopolitical event caused one commodity to respond in line with historical trading patterns, while another did not. On February 28, 2026, U.S. and Israeli forces began a military operation in Iran that continued throughout March, with Iran retaliating throughout the Middle East. Crude oil rallied from below $60 in late 2025 and early 2026 to over $100 per barrel. Rising crude oil prices have increased global inflationary pressures, with many analysts calling for even higher prices if the hostilities continue and escalate. Meanwhile, stocks and bonds fell as a risk-off environment led markets to trade amid the constantly changing news cycle.

Gold is typically an inflation barometer, but a rising U.S. dollar, elevated U.S. interest rates, and the price action over the past months that included substantial speculative buying, caused gold prices to post a double-digit percentage decline in March. The last time gold’s price fell by more than 10% in a month was in September 2011.

Gold enters April searching for a bottom, but the trend over the past twenty-seven years suggests that the market may have reached its nadir in March.

Gold falls by over 11% in March 2026- The other precious metals post declines

While selling descended on the gold market on January 29, 2026, and reached a low on February 2, 2026, gold recovered by the end of February. March was a different story as gold prices fell by more than 10%.

The daily continuous COMEX gold futures chart shows that gold’s price reached a record high of $5,626.80 on January 29 and fell 21.4% to $4,423.20 per ounce on February 2. However, gold settled January at $4,745.10, a 9.3% gain from the December 2025 closing price. The leading precious metal settled at $5,247.90 per ounce at the end of February, a 10.6% gain for the month. On March 31, 2026, nearby gold futures settled at $4,647.60 per ounce, an 11.43% loss for the month.

The other precious metals trading on the CME’s COMEX and NYMEX divisions posted double-digit percentage declines in March:

  • COMEX silver futures fell 19.7%.
  • NYMEX platinum futures declined by 18%.
  • NYMEX palladium futures moved 18.65% lower.

Despite gold’s 11.4% decline, it was the sector’s best performer in March.

Gold posted its 10th consecutive record high in Q1 2026

The end of March was also the end of the first quarter of 2026.

The quarterly continuous contract COMEX gold futures chart shows that gold posted its 10th consecutive quarterly new record high in Q1 2026. Despite gold’s 11.5% decline in March 2026, the leading precious metal still closed 7.41% higher than at the end of Q4 2025.

A bull market since the turn of this century

Gold’s bull market is nothing new, as it has made higher lows and higher highs since reaching a bottom at $252.80 per ounce in Q3 1999.

The quarterly chart dating back to the 1970s highlights the ascent of gold’s price from the 1999 low to the latest Q1 2026 high. Gold has moved over 22 times higher from the 1999 bottom to the 2026 peak.

Every correction has been a golden buying opportunity- The reasons for gold’s current correction

The long-term chart shows that over the past 27 years, every correction in gold’s price has been a buying opportunity, with new highs following.

Gold’s current price weakness follows a parabolic move in 2025 and January 2026 that more than doubled the price from the end of 2024. Gold’s rally attracted substantial investment and speculative buying during the rally.

Open interest is the total number of open long and short positions in a futures market. Open interest changes can provide clues about investor and trader activity.

The daily chart of COMEX June gold futures shows that total open interest fell by 33.2% from 541,033 contracts on January 22, before gold reached its latest record high, to 361,409 contracts on March 31 after gold prices declined. The open interest decline was likely due to substantial long liquidation as gold prices declined. In a futures market, falling prices and declining open interest are not necessarily a bearish sign.

Gold’s decline has cleaned out weaker market participants holding long positions, which could mean the March 23, 2026, low of $4,100 per ounce on the continuous COMEX futures contract could mark a significant bottom for the gold market if the trend of higher lows and higher highs continues.

The factors that support a continuation of the over two and a half decade rally include:

  • Central banks continue to add gold to reserves, validating gold’s growing role in the global financial system. At its peak, the value of gold reserves held by governments exceeded the value of euro and U.S. dollar reserve currency holdings.
  • While the U.S. dollar remains the world’s fiat reserve currency, de-dollarization because of sanctions, tariffs, and the bifurcation of the world’s nuclear powers supports continued gold accumulation.
  • Gold is a rare metal that has a unique role as an industrial commodity, an ornamental attraction, and a financial asset.
  • Gold remains in a long-term bullish trend, and the trend is always a trader or investor’s best friend.

The bottom line is that gold may have formed another new bottom and technical support level at $4,100 per ounce in March 2026, with higher highs on the horizon. Gold will need to trade over $5,626.80 per ounce, close to $1,000 above the end of March closing level, to establish its 11th consecutive record quarterly high before the end of June 2026. Another quarterly new high is a tall order for the gold market, but it is not impossible in the current environment.

GLD is the leading gold ETF, but IAU and BAR also own physical gold bullion- Leave room to add on any further declines

The most direct route to invest in or take a speculative position in gold is through the physical market for bars or coins. However, physical gold purchases often involve premiums for buyers and discounts for sellers.

COMEX gold futures have a delivery mechanism but require margin and use unique regulated trading accounts. Three gold ETFs that purport to own physical gold bullion are:

The SPDR Gold Shares (GLD): The GLD ETF is the oldest and most successful commodity ETF, introduced in November 2004. At $430.29 per ounce at the end of Q1, GLD had $155.11 billion in assets under management. On average, GLD trades over 14.95 million shares per day and charges a 0.40% management fee.

Seeking Alpha’s ETF Grades for GLD include the highest possible A+ grades in the momentum and liquidity categories. The management fee receives a B- grade, while the highly volatile gold market earns a C+ in the risk category.

The iShares Gold Trust ETF (IAU): The IAU ETF was introduced in January 2005. At $88.16 per ounce at the end of Q1, IAU had $70.52 billion in assets under management. IAU trades an average of ~15.9 million shares per day and charges a 0.25% management fee.

Seeking Alpha’s ETF Grades for IAU include the highest possible A+ grades in the momentum and liquidity categories. The management fee receives a B+ grade, slightly better than GLD’s due to IAU’s lower expense ratio. The highly volatile gold market earns a C+ in the risk category.

The Granite Shares Gold Trust (BAR): The BAR ETF began trading in August 2017. At $46.13 per ounce at the end of Q1, BAR had $1.59 billion in assets under management. BAR trades an average of ~522,000 shares per day and charges a 0.17% management fee.

Seeking Alpha’s ETF Grades for BAR include the highest possible A+ grades in the momentum category. BAR’s lower assets under management and daily trading volumes receive an A- in the liquidity category, lower than the highly liquid GLD and IAU ETFs. The management fee receives an A- grade, which is better than GLD and IAU due to BAR’s lower expense ratio. The highly volatile gold market earns a C+ in the risk category.

GLD, IAU, and BAR all do an excellent job of tracking gold prices because they hold 100% of their AUM in physical gold. I remain bullish on gold’s prospects in early April 2026 and believe the $4,100 level could mark a significant price bottom. However, it is virtually impossible to pick bottoms in any market during corrective periods, and gold is no exception. While I rate gold and the GLD, IAU, and BAR ETFs a buy in early April 2026, I suggest leaving room to add if the current correction takes prices lower over the coming weeks and months.

data.title: HHH Stock: 38% Valuation Discount Vs. Negative Growth Forecast | 2-Minute Analysis
data.description:

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Today we are diving into Howard Hughes Holdings, ticker symbol HHH. We're going to kick things off here with the Quant Rating system, which currently has a HOLD rating on this REIT. And we're going to dive into the metrics here in just a moment. Jumping over to the Seeking Alpha analysts, they currently have a HOLD in aggregate and that's from two analysts providing their coverage in the last 90 days.

Now lastly, Wall Street analysts currently have a BUY rating on this stock, and that's three analysts providing their coverage in the last 90 days. To learn more about how the Seeking Alpha Quant system and Seeking Alpha analysts outperform the market, visit the link in the description of this video.

So let's dive deeper. This is a $3.68 billion market capitalization REIT found within the real estate sector and the real estate development industry.

Let's jump on into the valuation grade currently a B+. First metric to highlight here is the price to the AFFO forward is currently 8.87 compared to the sector at 14.45. A nice 38.64% discount. Secondly, highlighting price-to-book ratio here for the trailing 12 months has been 0.97 compared to the sector at 1.47.

Now jumping over to the growth grade it is a D+. The revenue growth forward is unfortunately a negative 5.59% compared to the sector which is up 3.8%. And if we scroll up here a little bit you will see that the EPS diluted growth forward is -4.23% compared to the sector which is up 5.09%.

Jumping into profitability it is currently a C grade. Clocking in that gross profit margin trailing 12 months is 47.69% compared to the sector at 66.81%. And cash from operations is $462.37 million compared to the sector at $273.69 million. So HHH is outperforming the sector 68.94% on that cash from operations metrics.

Jumping into momentum it is currently a D grade. It’s been a rough year for HHH, down negative 17.46%, which compared to the sector is only down 5.51%. And even more recently on the 6-month and the 3-month price performance you’ll see that this REIT has taken quite a beating.

Jumping into the revisions grade here is currently an A- grade. That is zero up revisions and one down revision for earnings per share over the last three months, and zero up revisions and one down revision for revenue numbers over the last three months. So come on over here to Seeking Alpha, follow Howard Hughes Holdings here. You'll get all the free breaking news alerts. Obviously, people have been watching this stock for quite a while, Bill Ackman was involved at one point, still kind of is with Pershing Square. Keep an eye on this.

And that's going to wrap it up for this episode. If you have a ticker you want us to cover, add it to the comment section below. And don't forget to follow Seeking Alpha so you get notified when the next 2-Minute Analysis gets published.

data.title: Cal-Maine Foods: Pricing Collapse Crushes EPS, But Diversifying Limits Damage
data.description:
After a massive surge in egg pricing throughout 2025 driven by supply disruptions, Cal-Maine Foods, Inc. (CALM) stock enjoyed a significant run-up that we believed was borrowing from future gains. We cautioned that as the national hen flock recovered, a pricing reset was inevitable. While the stock has shown some resilience lately, the just-reported fiscal Q3 2026 results confirm that the peak of the cycle is firmly in the rearview mirror. Shares have fluctuated on the news of a bottom-line beat, but we believe the massive drop in top-line revenue suggests the stock may still be ahead of itself.

Having traded this stock through multiple cycles of avian influenza and commodity spikes, we can tell you it remains a classic trading vehicle subject to violent swings. We suspect the next meaningful move is lower as the market continues to price in normalized margins. Longer term, management is doing the right things by diversifying into prepared foods and specialty eggs, which should theoretically smooth out the earnings volatility. However, at the current valuation, the stock remains a better trading name than a long-term buy and hold in our opinion. It appears cheap, but that is only if margins can hold up, and that largely depends on where egg pricing goes. As such are have a Hold rating here and suspect the nearer-term direction is sideways.

The fiscal Q3 results show a company successfully pivoting its product mix, but one that is still being hammered by a collapse in conventional egg prices. Let us discuss.

Cal-Maine Foods Fiscal Q3 sales and volumes

The company reported net sales of $667 million, an eye-popping 53% drop from the $1.4 billion seen in the same quarter last year. This decline was almost entirely driven by a 72.1% collapse in conventional egg revenue. While the top line was poor, things were actually better than expected as analyst sentiment and expectations were very beaten down. The company actually beat consensus earnings estimates by reporting $1.06 in EPS, though this was still down nearly 90% from the prior year.

The key driver here is a normalization in pricing. According to the USDA, the national layer flock has increased to roughly 308 million hens, and depopulations due to highly pathogenic avian influenza were down substantially year-over-year. This increase in supply has crushed wholesale prices.

While conventional volumes fell about 6.7%, the company is seeing success in its specialty egg segment. Specialty eggs now account for 50.5% of total shell egg sales, up significantly from 24.4% a year ago. We like this shift, as specialty eggs generally command more stable pricing and better margins. Furthermore, the company is seeing an explosion in its prepared foods business, which saw sales rise 441% to $63.6 million this quarter. This was largely driven by the acquisition of Echo Lake and increased production from Crepini. CEO Sherman Miller noted that the quarter provided a real-time test of this diversification strategy, and while volumes are growing in these subcategories, they were not enough to offset the massive price deflation in the core conventional market.

Recent acquisitions and margins

The company has stayed busy on the acquisition front, recently announcing the purchase of certain assets from Creighton Brothers LLC and Crystal Lake. This move is designed to expand their geographic reach and increase internal sourcing for egg-based ingredients. From a margin perspective, gross profit fell to $119.3 million from $716.1 million last year. Gross margin sat at 17.9%, which is a far cry from the historic levels seen during the supply crunch.

However, feed costs remain a bright spot. Similar to what we saw in previous quarters, the price of corn and soybean meal has trended lower, which provided a much-needed tailwind to help the company beat earnings expectations despite the revenue miss.

Cal-Maine Foods' variable dividend

Shareholders should remember that the dividend here is variable and tied directly to quarterly net income. The policy dictates a payout of one-third of net income. For fiscal Q3, the company declared a dividend of approximately $0.36 per share. While this is a positive for those holding the stock, it is a massive step down from the payouts seen during the 2025 peak. For income seekers, this lack of predictability makes the stock difficult to own as a core holding, though the company’s rock-solid balance sheet and lack of debt remain impressive. We also note that the company repurchased over 329,000 shares during the quarter, showing that management is committed to returning capital even as the cycle turns.

Final thoughts

It really is all about the commodity cycle for Cal-Maine Foods, Inc., and right now the cycle is in a clear cooling phase. We give management credit for the aggressive push into prepared foods and specialty products, which accounted for over 52% of net sales this quarter. This was not deemed a pivot according to management, but effectively is a big diversification strategy. As such, unlike the past when egg prices would collapse, shares may not fall as hard as they have in the past, which is why we have a Hold/neutral on the name rather than a Sell rating.

Still, EPS is expected to continue falling for the rest of this fiscal year and next fiscal year. While that is a headwind for the stock, this shift will eventually lead to more durable earnings, but in the near term, the collapse in conventional egg pricing is the dominant narrative. We view the stock as fairly valued here given the EPS declines ahead, and maintain our neutral bias. A $70 and lower share price to us would seem to be a better buy.

data.title: Sportradar Group AG: Both The Moat And Growth Outlook Have Improved
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Sportradar Group AG (SRAD) was a buy rating because I was confident in the growth ahead as the drivers were visible. I also believed SRAD had strong pricing power. As for this update, I am staying bullish on SRAD because it now has more ways to monetize, and its moat has gotten stronger. IMG strengthens the platform, AI adds product and margin upside, and Playradar opens another growth driver.

IMG

Relative to my initiation coverage, I think the biggest development in the thesis is IMG. As a quick recap, my view on SRAD’s moat is that it sits in the middle of the ecosystem, has deep integrations, and benefits from operators not wanting to manage a disparate set of vendors (for data, odds, streaming, and trading tools). The IMG acquisition makes SRAD’s moat a lot stronger. For context, the acquired portfolio includes relationships with >70 rights holders, ~38,000 official data events, and ~29,000 streaming events across 14 global sports on six continents. That is a massive boost to what SRAD already has. Post the deal, SRAD’s sports coverage will increase to >1 million matches annually, and the benefits are obvious. Now, SRAD has more data to sell, more streaming to distribute, and more inventory to work with. But I think an important point is how management integrates IMG into the existing product set. Specifically, they said IMG content is being integrated into products such as Foresight, micro markets, player props, and the Virtual Live Match Tracker. Which means IMG is not operated as a standalone side product; it is being well integrated so that clients will not face the friction of “adopting another product" and at the same time reap the benefits of this IMG acquisition, which ultimately gives SRAD justification to step up monetization.

The rate at which IMG is being “monetized” is also very positive. Management said the majority of clients, including all Tier 1 partners, have already signed on for IMG data, odds, and audiovisual products. They also said SRAD is on track to unlock revenue synergies of 25% for IMG in 2026, which tells us how well IMG fits the platform. That is why I think IMG is such an important development in the thesis. SRAD’s current moat is so much stronger and deeper, such that it makes the platform harder to replicate and more valuable to existing customers.

AI

A new narrative that emerged is AI, which I think is beneficial for SRAD. I think SRAD benefits in three ways. First, it improves data collection. Management said historically ~50% of its data has been collected through computer vision, and with AI, that is moving toward 75% to 80% over time. Second, it helps SRAD to create better new products. For example, the basketball foundation model is able to predict what is likely to happen next in a game, which makes richer betting and engagement tools possible. Third, it can also lower internal costs by helping to handle large volumes of data requests and supporting back-end functions.

A product example here is the customized 4Sight product for NBC Universal Peacock’s NBA coverage. This is an AI-powered live-streaming product that overlays real-time stats, visual cues, and actionable insights directly onto live video. This effectively turns a standard stream into a more interactive viewing experience. Take the case of basketball: 4Sight will process a very large volume of official data to generate animated overlays and insights that can help predict likely outcomes in a game, which in turn can support richer fan engagement and better real-time betting decisions. In other words, this is a customer-facing product layer that can make SRAD’s content more valuable and more monetizable. Management even said that many of the underlying costs are already locked in, which means the incremental margins could be massive once SRAD starts ramping up these AI products.

iGaming

At the March conference, management launched Playradar as a dedicated iGaming brand. The idea is to connect sportsbook and casino experiences through hybrid content (including virtual sports and casino products such as slots, table games, arcade games, and crash games). First of all, I think this is a move that carries very little risk. SRAD is not entering this market from scratch. SRAD already works with 800 sportsbooks; 70% to 80% of those partners also have an iGaming component, and it already delivers 500,000 video streams annually to sportsbook partners. This means SRAD already has an installed base, distribution, and content capabilities. Secondly, the TAM is huge. Management sized the iGaming market at >$100 billion and said it does not need to spend heavily upfront or hurt margins to pursue the opportunity. We still have to see how well SRAD can execute here, but if it goes well, this could become a huge growth driver.

Valuation

The upside is even more attractive today than the last time I wrote about this. Both FY25 revenue growth and adj. EBITDA margin came in better than I expected, and management FY26 guidance is very bullish, calling for 22% y/y revenue growth and adj. EBITDA margin expanding to 25%. With this, I am upgrading my growth assumption from mid-teens to 20% for FY27 and FY28. I keep my adj. EBITDA is the same at 26%, which translates to adj. EBITDA growth of >20%. If SRAD does as I expect, I believe the current 10x forward EBITDA multiple is too low relative to history. I still think SRAD deserves to trade back to its historical average of 18x since its adj. EBITDA growth is still strong. It is unlikely we see SRAD trade back to 25x, like it did in early-to-mid 2025 when adj. EBITDA growth was >30%. But at >20%, I think 18x is a fair multiple.

Risk

I think the key risk now is execution. There are now a couple of key moving parts in the business: integrating IMG, pushing into iGaming, and trying to monetize newer products like 4Sight. I am hopeful of how they would play out, but if those revenue streams scale slower than expected while sports rights costs keep rising, margin expansion could disappoint.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating for SRAD. The moat is getting stronger, and there are more ways to grow now. IMG should deepen customer value, AI can lift monetization and margins, and Playradar adds another leg of growth with limited upfront risk. If management executes well from here, I think the current valuation still looks too low for a business that can grow adj. EBITDA at >20% over the next few years.

data.title: Cadre Holdings: Nuclear Stock Meltdown Creates Upgraded Strong Buy Opportunity
data.description:
Cadre Holdings (CDRE) stock has tumbled nearly 25% since my last report, substantially underperforming the S&P 500. The price return is extremely disappointing for a company on which I have a buy rating and with exposure to the law enforcement and nuclear markets. Cadre Holdings has ventured into the nuclear markets through acquisitions, but we are now also seeing that the nuclear market also holds its own set of headwinds apart from the positive growth characteristics.

In this report, I discuss the company’s Q4 2025 and explain why I believe the stock is now a strong buy.

Cadre Holdings Investor Day Lacks Useful Detail

Cadre Holdings posted a 5% decline in net sales, which was definitely disappointing, as analysts had anticipated around 4% growth. Gross margins slipped 50 basis points, while net income declined from $13 million to $11.7 million or from $0.32 per share to $0.27 per share. Adjusted EBITDA declined nearly 11% to $34.4 million, driven by the 5% decline in sales and a 1.3 point decline in adjusted EBITDA margins.

While I do like the positioning of Cadre as a company with exposure to the law enforcement and nuclear markets, in my view they have always fallen short in providing useful insights into the growth drivers. Especially when markets are in a risk-off mode and financial results fall behind expectations, it can cause significant pressure on the share price performance.

The positive was that the reason for the miss was primarily due to contracts shifting from 2025 into 2026. So it is not a loss of contracts but the timing of contracts. However, there is also a more structural issue, and that is that the US policy is shifting away from plutonium downblending, and that reduces demand for the products of Alpha Safety, which the company acquired in 2024 and is a high-margin business for Cadre Holdings.

Cadre Holdings acquired Alpha Safety for $106.5 million in 2024 funded with cash on hand and debt. With the business coming from Alpha Safety now being softer than initially expected, I do believe that investors are wondering whether Cadre Holdings didn’t pay too much for Alpha Safety. Especially for Cadre Holdings, which sees little low-to mid-single-digit organic growth supplemented by $100 million in annual M&A, the possible fumble on Alpha Safety may have investors take a more cautious stance.

Cadre Holdings Aims For Strong Growth In 2026

For 2026, Cadre Holdings is targeting 22.4% growth in sales at the midpoint of the $736-$758 million range, with 24% growth in adjusted EBITDA with a guided range of $136-$141 million. That points at margins of around 18.5%. Organic growth is set to accelerate from 2% in 2025 to 3%-5%. The main growth contributor is the acquisition of TYR Tactical, which has an annual run rate of $100 million with an expected contribution this year of high $80 million to low $90 million at margins of 20%. The acquisition of TYR Tactical was finalized in February this year for $120 million in cash, $24 million in stock and $1 million restricted stock units with an additional $25 million earn-out consideration. TYR Tactical provides military gear, plate carriers and tactical equipment, making it a fit for Cadre’s law enforcement business.

Cadre Holdings Stock Upgraded To Strong Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Cadre Holdings, I believe that there is 61% upside to $49.27 based on forward earnings and even 88% when valuing the stock another year ahead. The stock remains undervalued against peers, which trade at an EV/EBITDA of nearly 21x. Sales growth is expected to be 12.2% annually through 2028, with 17.7% annual growth in EBITDA and 23.1% annual growth in free cash flow, while also modeling in around $100 million in acquisitions. The challenging part is that I already modelled in this M&A activity, but the exact inorganic upside for revenues, EBITDA and FCF depends on what company will be acquired and the closing data of the acquisitions. Nonetheless, I do believe that the recent stock price decline offers a compelling entry point, and as a result, I am upgrading the stock to strong buy with a $49.27 price target as the base case, providing 61% upside, and $55.22 in a more bullish scenario, providing 80% upside.

The expectation is that in the years ahead, operating leverage will increase through a combination of organic and inorganic growth, pushing EBITA margins north of 21%. We also note that free cash flow is expected to significantly increase, and while I do believe that the company will continue to fund M&A through a combination of debt and cash on hand, it will also be better positioned to fund more of its acquisitions through cash flows. We do note that the company has $260.4 million in debt maturing in 2029 (10K, Page 71), which it could technically cover through expected free cash flow for 2029 in combination with cash on hand, but I do believe that the company will refinance some of its debt, if not all. One risk that remains is that some acquisitions are financed with stock.

Conclusion: Cadre Holdings Is Looking More Attractive

Cadre Holdings stock substantially declined due to fourth-quarter revenues slipping into 2026, with plutonium downblending demand seemingly facing a more structural demand pressure. However, I do believe that the stock price decline is somewhat overdone, offering an attractive entry point for investors interested in a company that is highly active in the M&A space. As a result, I am upgrading the stock to strong buy.

data.title: Sigma Lithium: Turning Into A Cash Machine
data.description:
Sigma Lithium Corporation (SGML) got a big boost this week after the lithium miner altered the cash flow picture. The company is benefitting from higher lithium prices (LC:COM) and an ongoing realization that growth drivers remain strong despite ongoing weakness in the U.S. EV market. My investment thesis is Bullish on the stock due to the strong expansion plans without the need to raise additional capital.

Major Offtake Agreements

Sigma Lithium produces the Quintuple Zero lithium with zero drinking water, dirty power, or hazardous chemicals used in the process to produce lithium. The biggest question with the business has been the cash position, especially last year when lithium prices got so low.

The lithium miner has now signed multiple volume commitments with $146 million in pre-payments for offtake agreements to solve the financial picture. Sigma Lithium is now on a path to a much stronger future after ending 2025 with a cash balance of only $6 million.

The company forecasts Q1 '26 (the quarter that just ended) cash inflows of $35 million, followed by a large $96 million payment in Q2. The company announced the major $96 million prepayment for 70,500 tons of production in 2026.

Sigma Lithium plans another offtake agreement for 80K tons annually with a $100 million prepayment. The company plans to use this prepayment to repay some of the long-term debt currently outstanding at $140 million.

Expansion Plans

Over the course of the next few years, Sigma plans to expand the production volumes by expanding from 1 plant to 3 plants. The goal is for annual lithium production to triple from 240,000 tpa to 770,000 tpa in 2028.

Based on forecasted costs dipping to $495/t, down from $592/t now, with $60/t assigned to interest expense reductions, Sigma Lithium projects the potential to produce $900 million in operating cash flows based on nameplate capacity goals for 2028 with lithium prices at $2,000/t.

The company repaying debt via the prepayments helps reduce a good portion of the cost reductions planned over the next couple of years. Sigma Lithium plans scale and operating efficiencies to help reduce the additional costs.

The lithium miner is only forecasting $80 million to build Plant 2 to boost production volumes to 520,000/t in 2027 and another $100 million for Plant 3 to further increase production to 770,000/t in 2028. Sigma Lithium only needs to spend $180 million total on capex, with the potential for annual cash flows to far exceed the amount of money spent to build these plants. For this reason, management doesn't plan to raise any additional capital despite a low cash balance to start the year.

The stock currently has a market cap of just $1.3 billion. If one has a positive view on lithium prices, Sigma Lithium could be producing cash flows closer to the market cap in a few years, and the company used the Q4 '25 earnings call to point out this discrepancy.

The company is currently forecasting quarterly sales in the range of $48 million, but naturally lithium prices play a huge part in actual sales totals. The consensus analyst estimates forecast revenues reaching $500 million in 2027, which, of course, appears very conservative considering Sigma Lithium forecasts cash flows of up to $500 million based on current lithium price realizations around the $1,800/t level.

The stock has limited analyst coverage, so the opportunity is for additional coverage to boost the visibility of Sigma Lithium. As with all lithium producers, the downside risk is lower lithium prices, with the stock trading at $5 just months ago.

The ultimate risk is for global use of lithium to not grow while these additional supplies come online. The EV battery and battery storage markets could always develop a better battery technology that replaces lithium.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Sigma Lithium still appears far too cheap with the projected cash flows. The stock traded up above $40 back in 2023 when lithium prices were much higher, and the opportunity exists to return to such levels on higher lithium production without the need for higher prices.

Investors should continue buying Sigma Lithium before the cash flow picture is fully understood.

data.title: I'm Not Buying Tuesday's 'Hopium' Rally
data.description:
Equities staged a rip-your-face-off rally on Tuesday to end a dismal first quarter for investors on a high note. The NASDAQ led the advance with better than a 3.8% rise, the index's best daily performance since May of last year. The small-cap Russell 2000 was up better than 3.4%, and the Dow and S&P 500 climbed more than 2% on the day. Hopes for a cessation of hostilities in the Middle East were the impetus for the rally.

Interestingly, WTI oil rose a bit over a buck a barrel Tuesday despite rising hopes the effective closure in the Strait of Hormuz would be lifted. Brent crude did fall over 4% on the day. To be fair, both WTI and Brent oil prices are falling some early Wednesday. In addition, the POTUS is scheduled to address the country later today about this conflict. One has to assume the administration would not have booked airtime if there wasn't good news to report.

Obviously, an ending to the hostilities in the Middle East and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz will trigger a huge sigh of relief from global investors. A further rally in the equity markets is also highly likely. However, I will not be buying this rally. I will use the rebound in equities to "roll" some of the options on the covered call positions in my portfolio that don't look like they will expire in the money from April to June to pick up additional premiums. Other than that, my trading action will be limited. My portfolio will remain roughly 30% in short-term Treasuries and cash and 70% within covered call holdings around the few stocks in the market I am finding with reasonable valuations.

And that is because I am not convinced that we are nearly out of the woods on several fronts. First, even if the Strait of Hormuz opened fully by the end of the week, it will take weeks for smelters, refineries, and chemical plants in the region to restart fully. In addition, many assets in the region were damaged or destroyed completely and will take months and years to bring back online. A massive Qatar LNG facility took some significant damage that could take five years to fully restore.

And even if oil, LNG, phosphate, urea, nitrogen, aluminum, etc. start to flow freely through this global chokepoint, prices for these products will remain significantly higher than they were before this conflict for many months, if not throughout the rest of 2026. Large oil reserves were released in Europe and the United States, and they will need to be replenished over time. Other commodities will need to be restocked, and some production will be lost forever. In addition, insurance rates for tankers and carriers traversing the Strait of Hormuz are likely to be much higher than they were before this regional war.

As I noted the other day, residential real estate is off to a rough start to 2026 after three years of moribund existing home sales. January new home sales showed a large drop, and average mortgage rates remain above those of September 2024, when the Fed started to take down the Fed Funds rate by a cumulative 175bps to date. Home sellers outnumber home buyers by the largest percentage since Redfin started to keep records in 2013.

Commercial real estate, or CRE, is in even worse shape. Trepp came out Tuesday with their March look at Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities, or CMBS. The overall CMBS delinquency rate for March rose 41bps month-over-month to 7.55%. The CMBS delinquency rate for office properties (some $1.3 trillion in debt outstanding) moved up to nearly 12% in March. This is considerably higher than the peak in the Great Financial Crisis, it should be noted. Roughly $600 billion in office-based CRE needs to be refinanced in 2026 and 2027. Office values have collapsed in many major cities thanks to factors like the explosion in the virtual workforce following Covid.

CMBS delinquency rates also surged above 7% for lodging and multi-family properties. The latter sector is quite important, as there is some $2.2 trillion of debt outstanding. Apartment vacancy rates are at their highest levels since records started to be tracked on this metric, and national rents have fallen nationally over six of the last eight months. Of note, the CMBS delinquency rates for all three of these sectors were under 2% before Chairman Powell started to lift the Fed Funds rate in March 2022.

Then, we have the deteriorating situation in private credit, where a new "cockroach" seemed to emerge every few days in the month of March. Many major private credit funds "gated" redemptions in March, including Apollo Global Management (APO), Cliffwater, Morgan Stanley (MS), and BlackRock (BLK). JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) marked down the collateral used to lend money to the private credit sector in March, which sent a shot across the bow in this space, as I noted in this recent article.

The consumer was struggling before the recent spike in gasoline and diesel prices in March thanks to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz. With revisions, job growth only averaged 15,000 jobs a month in 2025. February saw a 92,000-job loss, according to the BLS. Oracle (ORCL) is one of several companies laying off a significant percentage of their workforce because of AI, joining other notable names like Block (XYZ) and Meta Platforms (META).

Delinquencies across the roughly $4.5 trillion of combined debt that consumers hold in student, credit card, and auto loans have been on the rise. One new factoid came to my attention this week that highlights the challenging conditions consumers face. 30.5% of buyers trading in a car in March toward a new vehicle are carrying negative equity. This is according to a JD Power report that came out last week. This metric has seen a steady rise since the Federal Reserve started to tighten monetary policy in the first quarter of 2022. This is up 4.2% from the same period in 2025, of note. In addition, consumers were on track to purchase $49.6 billion worth of new vehicles in March. This is down nearly 14% from March 2025. The average cost of a new vehicle in March was north of $49,000.

Valuations and breadth in the market are hardly compelling even after equities decline in the first quarter. The Shiller PE ratio has come down some since the first of the year due to losses in the stock market year to date. However, it remains substantially elevated at 37.6x. To put it in perspective, this is roughly 10 points higher than this metric was in 2007, prior to the subprime crisis unfolding.

Finally, while the three major indexes are no longer in correction territory after Tuesday's big rally, they are covering for weakness throughout the market. At the beginning of the week, according to Morgan Stanley, some 42% of S&P 500 members were already down 20% or more from their 52-week highs. Not exactly encouraging breadth when over 40% of the S&P 500 are experiencing bear markets.

Therefore, I view the huge rise in equities on Tuesday as a "hopium" rally. I remain cautious on the overall market given the issues on myriad fronts as well as extreme valuation levels and discouraging breadth.

data.title: Freshworks: Value Is Becoming Difficult To Ignore (Upgrade)
data.description:
We've completed a bruising first quarter of 2026, and so far, the enterprise software sector remains at historic lows, dogged by a "SaaSpocalypse" narrative that continues to persist despite relatively strong results by many companies in the space. While I think we're not likely to see any near-term recovery in multiples, long-term-oriented investors have a fantastic opportunity to buy many stocks here at a deep discount.

Companies like Freshworks (FRSH), in particular, have become quite compelling buys against bottom-line metrics. While I can't argue with the fact that a tough macro is putting pressure against growth rates, we have to ask: How much of that risk is already priced in, after Freshworks' ~30% decline since the start of the year?

I last wrote a "Neutral" article on Freshworks in November, when the stock was trading at $12 per share. Since then, Freshworks has seen its share price erode by roughly one-third. We won't disagree that execution has been a sticky point recently, with the company shedding total customers (though large customer counts continue to grow). At the same time, we're also observing meaningful bottom-line expansion, which reduces our reliance on revenue multiples to justify Freshworks' share price. With all of these factors weighed, I'm raising my rating on the stock to a "Buy."

In my view, these are the top reasons to be long on Freshworks:

  • Complex software that is largely AI-resistant. As a reminder, Freshworks' core products automate customer service, employee/customer experience, and sales/CRM tools. Each of these products is integrated into companies' daily operations, with integrations into third-party systems that make ripping out Freshworks and replacing it with AI solutions difficult. The company's recent buildup in large customers suggests that more customers are viewing the space as better to "buy" than to "DIY build."
  • Upmarket push and reduced reliance on smaller customers. Freshworks has been landing larger clients, especially with its Employee Experience (EX) software suite. The company has greater potential to cross-sell into larger companies. Over 60% of the company's ARR is generated from enterprise and mid-market clients, which gives the company revenue stability amid macroeconomic downturns.
  • Growth/profitability balance. Though not quite a "Rule of 40" software company, we note that Freshworks balances mid-teens revenue growth against a near-20% pro forma operating margin, still positioning Freshworks above a "Rule of 30" as it works to improve its bottom-line margins.
  • Strong balance sheet. Freshworks has nearly $0.8 billion of cash on its balance sheet, unencumbered by debt. This represents a large chunk of the stock's current market cap, enabling buybacks and potential growth acquisitions.

The top reason to "Buy" this stock remains valuation. At current share prices just above $8, Freshworks trades at a market cap of $2.27 billion. Netting off the $781.4 million of cash on the company's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $1.49 billion.

For FY26, as shown in the chart below, Freshworks is guiding to $952-$960 million in revenue, or 14% midpoint growth. If we assume FCF grows in line with revenue at 14% y/y (and at a ~27% adjusted FCF margin), FY26 FCF would be $254.3 million.

This positions Freshworks' valuation at:

  • 1.6x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 5.9x EV/FY26 FCF
  • 14.5x FY26 P/E, or 9.5x ex-cash P/E

These are appealing bottom-line multiples. While we do note that Freshworks has issues in sales execution as well as dilution (stock comp of $147 million in FY25 made up roughly two-thirds of the year's cash flow), we think the company's combination of recurring revenue, upmarket customer push, and improving profitability make this a compelling long-term investment.

Stay long here and use this year's dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Freshworks' latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Freshworks' revenue grew 14% y/y to $222.7 million, slightly ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $218.7 million (+12% y/y). Revenue growth decelerated slightly relative to 15% y/y growth in Q3.

One of the core issues with Freshworks' recent performance has been sluggish customer growth. As shown below, the company's y/y growth in customer counts slowed to just 3% y/y growth, while total customers of 74.5k shed ~400 customers relative to 74.9k as of the end of Q3.

That said, Freshworks has been intent on landing larger deals recently, and it's deprioritizing its smaller SMB clients. Though the company lost customers as a whole, the client segment of customers who generate more than >$50k in ARR grew by 148 sequentially to 3,760 such customers, up 23% y/y.

Speaking further to Freshworks' upmarket push on the Q4 earnings call, CEO Dennis Woodside commented as follows:

We saw an upmarket momentum surge with our enterprise cohorts outpacing overall growth, proving our ability to consistently win and scale within the world's most complex organizations. As of Q4, we now have over 1,500 customers with greater than $100,000 in ARR, an increase of 28% year-over-year and over 3,700 customers with greater than $50,000 in ARR, an increase of 23% year-over-year. For our first strategic priority in employee experience, we crossed the $0.5 billion milestone as of the end of 2025, reaching $510 million in ARR. That represents 26% year-over-year growth on an as-reported basis and 22% year-over-year on a constant currency basis.

Now we are witnessing a generational shift where midsized and larger enterprise organizations expect sophisticated software that can handle their complex needs and get fast time to value. Freshservice is uniquely positioned to fill this gap left by legacy providers like ServiceNow.

We like the fact that Freshworks is remaining in net expansion territory, with net dollar retention at 108% in the quarter (with some benefit from FX; on a constant-currency basis, net retention was 104%, indicating the average customer boosted their spending on the Freshworks platform by 4% y/y).

We are wary of the fact that the company has been spending a good deal more on sales and marketing lately. Pro forma sales and marketing costs, excluding stock comp, rose to 43% of revenue in Q4, up from 39% in the year-ago quarter. This had the impact of blunting operating margins, down to 18.7% in Q4 (-200bps y/y).

That said, we do note that the company is intent on fixing its go-to-market issues. In March, the company announced a streamlined global sales org, letting go of its "chief customer growth officer" and unifying sales leadership under a new Chief Revenue Officer position. We're hopeful that the company's intent to address sales performance will lead to positive deal momentum in FY26.

Free cash flow in Q4 rose 35% y/y to $56.2 million. On a full-year basis, the company generated $223.1 million in free cash flow, up 46% y/y and hitting a 26.6% FCF margin that improved 530bps y/y.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are also risks at play here that we need to keep in mind. As previously mentioned, many investors are wary of Freshworks' stock comp, which at $147 million in FY25 constituted the majority of the company's free cash flow. That said, there is a self-regulating mechanism here in Freshworks' declining share prices; we expect RSU values to diminish and many employee options to expire without value due to the large declines in Freshworks' share price since the start of the year, which should reduce dilution in FY26. We note as well that the recent sales leadership transition could drive near-term friction in deal momentum.

We do find that these risks are fairly priced into the stock, especially with Freshworks trading at a <10x ex-cash P/E multiple. Though we'll likely need to be patient to achieve a true upward multiples re-rating, the stock is now low enough to take a stake.
